# Exodus Chapter 20 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44v–45r (PDF pp. 85–86); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 61–62 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_020_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_020_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE DECALOGUE (ASERET HA-DIBROT, "THE TEN WORDS"):** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 20 across two pages in standard prose format with no special layout distinguishing the Decalogue from surrounding narrative, though the Masoretic tradition marks the chapter with distinctive cantillation — the ta'am elyon ("upper accentuation") system, which treats each commandment as a single verse unit for public liturgical reading, and the ta'am takhton ("lower accentuation") system, which preserves the standard verse division for study. This chapter is the theological summit of the Torah and arguably of the entire Hebrew Bible. The divine speech that began in 19:3 now reaches its climax: YHWH speaks directly to the entire assembled nation — not through Mosheh, not through an angel, but mouth to ear, God to people. The rabbis call these the Aseret ha-Dibrot, the "Ten Words" or "Ten Utterances" (not "Ten Commandments" — the Hebrew term dibrot, from דבר, davar, "word/thing/matter," is broader than "commandment"; these are divine declarations that encompass identity, relationship, and obligation simultaneously). The chapter divides into three sections: the Decalogue proper (vv. 1–17), with YHWH's self-identification (v. 2), commandments governing the divine-human relationship (vv. 3–11), and commandments governing human-human relationships (vv. 12–17); the people's terrified response and retreat (vv. 18–21); and the altar law (vv. 22–26), which begins the Covenant Code (Sefer ha-Berit) that extends through 23:33. The numbering of the "Ten" varies across Jewish, Catholic/Lutheran, and Reformed/Orthodox traditions — Judaism counts v. 2 ("I am YHWH your God") as the first "word"; Catholicism and Lutheranism combine vv. 3–6 and split v. 17 into two; the Reformed tradition treats vv. 3 and 4–6 as separate commandments. The EMET translation follows the MT verse numbering without imposing any traditional enumeration. The Vaticanus text (pp. 61–62) presents the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script; notably, the LXX order of the commandments in vv. 13–15 differs from the MT (the LXX of Codex Vaticanus reads: adultery, theft, murder — rather than the MT's murder, adultery, theft), reflecting an alternative tradition also attested in some NT quotations (Mark 10:19; Luke 18:20; Rom 13:9; Jas 2:11) and in Philo.

---

## The Decalogue: Divine Self-Identification (vv. 1–2)

**1** And God [Elohim] spoke [vaydabber] all [et-kol] these [ha-elleh] words [ha-devarim], saying [le'mor]:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְדַבֵּר אֱלֹהִים אֵת כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה לֵאמֹר (vaydabber Elohim et kol-ha-devarim ha-elleh le'mor, "and God spoke all these words, saying"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — DIVINE SPEECH TO THE NATION:** וַיְדַבֵּר (vaydabber) = piel of דבר (davar, "to speak, declare") — "and he spoke." The piel of davar is the standard form for formal, sustained speech (as opposed to אמר, amar, "to say," which introduces quotation). God speaks (yedabber) — not through Mosheh, not through thunder or fire, but in articulate, intelligible words. The subject is אֱלֹהִים (Elohim, "God") — not YHWH. The use of Elohim here (rather than YHWH, which appeared throughout chapter 19) is striking: Elohim is the universal divine title, emphasizing God as sovereign Creator and Judge of all. The commandments that follow are grounded not in the particular covenant name (YHWH) but in the universal authority of the God of all creation — though v. 2 immediately specifies YHWH. אֵת כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (et kol-ha-devarim ha-elleh) = "all these words" — the definite object marker אֵת (et) + כָּל (kol, "all") + הַדְּבָרִים (ha-devarim, "the words") + הָאֵלֶּה (ha-elleh, "these"). The term is devarim ("words/things/matters"), not mitsvot ("commandments") or khuqqim ("statutes"). These are "words" — utterances of divine will that are simultaneously acts of creation: the God who spoke the world into being (Gen 1) now speaks the moral order into being. לֵאמֹר (le'mor) = "saying" — the infinitive construct of אמר (amar), used formulaically to introduce direct speech. The rabbis debated whether God spoke all ten utterances simultaneously (since divine speech is not bound by temporal sequence) and then repeated them individually, or spoke them sequentially as human speech. LXX: καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πάντας τοὺς λόγους τούτους λέγων ("and the Lord spoke all these words, saying") — **LXX VARIANT:** κύριος (kyrios, "Lord") for Elohim — the LXX renders the speaker as "the Lord" rather than "God," conforming to its general practice of using kyrios for the divine name. λαλέω ("speak, talk") for vaydabber; λόγος ("word, account, reason") for devarim.

**2** "I [anokhi] [am] YHWH your God [Elohekha], who [asher] brought you out [hotsetikha] from the land [me-erets] of Mitsrayim [Egypt], from the house [mi-beit] of slaves [avadim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אָנֹכִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִיךָ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים (anokhi YHWH Elohekha asher hotsetikha me-erets Mitsrayim mi-beit avadim, "I [am] YHWH your God, who brought you out from the land of Mitsrayim, from the house of slaves"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE DIVINE SELF-IDENTIFICATION:** This verse is the preamble to the entire Decalogue and, in the Jewish tradition, the first of the Ten Words. It is not a command but a declaration of identity — God introduces Himself before making any demands. The structure mirrors ancient Near Eastern suzerainty treaties, where the great king identifies himself and recounts his benevolent acts before stipulating the vassal's obligations. אָנֹכִי (anokhi) = "I" — the emphatic first-person pronoun (more solemn and archaic than the common אֲנִי, ani). The first word God speaks to Yisra'el at Sinai is "I" — the self-assertion of divine personhood. God is not a force, a principle, or an abstraction; God is an "I" who addresses a "you." יְהוָה (YHWH) = the tetragrammaton, the covenant name of God, from the root היה (hayah, "to be"). The name revealed to Mosheh at the burning bush (3:14–15): אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה ("I AM WHO I AM" / "I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE"). YHWH is the God who IS — who exists absolutely, who causes to be, who is present with His people. אֱלֹהֶיךָ (Elohekha) = "your God" — Elohim + 2ms suffix. The address is singular (you, not you-all): God speaks to each individual Israelite personally, as well as to the nation collectively. The singular "you" persists throughout the Decalogue. אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִיךָ (asher hotsetikha) = "who brought you out" — hiphil of יצא (yatsa, "to go out") + 1cs + 2ms suffix — "who I-brought-you-out." The relative clause identifies YHWH not by metaphysical attributes (omnipotence, omniscience) but by historical action: this is the God who did something specific, in a specific place, at a specific time. Theology is rooted in history. מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם (me-erets Mitsrayim) = "from the land of Mitsrayim" — Egypt, the place of enslavement. מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים (mi-beit avadim) = "from the house of slaves" — בֵּית (bayit, "house") + עֲבָדִים (avadim, "slaves, servants," plural of עֶבֶד, eved, "slave, servant," from עבד, avad, "to serve, work"). The phrase beit avadim ("house of slaves" = "slave-house") is a recurring epithet for Egypt in Deuteronomy (5:6; 6:12; 8:14; 13:5, 10). The exodus defines YHWH's identity: He is the God who liberates. The commandments that follow are not the impositions of a tyrant but the guidelines of a liberator — the one who freed you from slavery now tells you how to live in freedom. The rabbis noted: "Why were the Ten Words not given at the beginning of the Torah? A parable: A king entered a province and said, 'Shall I reign over you?' They said, 'What have you done for us that you should reign over us?' He built them a wall, brought them water, fought their wars. Then he said, 'Shall I reign over you?' They said, 'Yes, yes!' So God brought Yisra'el out of Egypt, split the sea, gave them manna, gave them the well, brought them quail, fought Amaleq for them. Then He said, 'Shall I reign over you?' They said, 'Yes, yes!'" (Mekhilta de-Rabbi Yishmael). LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος ὁ θεός σου, ὅστις ἐξήγαγόν σε ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ἐξ οἴκου δουλείας ("I am the Lord your God, who led you out from the land of Egypt, from the house of slavery") — ἐγώ εἰμι ("I am") for anokhi — the same phrase that appears in the Gospel of John's "I AM" sayings; δουλεία ("slavery, bondage") for avadim — the LXX uses the abstract noun "slavery" rather than the concrete "slaves."

---

## The Decalogue: Commands Toward God (vv. 3–11)

**3** You shall not have [lo yihyeh lekha] other [acherim] gods [elohim] before my face [al-panai].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא יִהְיֶה־לְךָ אֱלֹהִים אֲחֵרִים עַל־פָּנָי (lo yihyeh-lekha elohim acherim al-panai, "there shall not be for you other gods upon/before my face"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE FIRST PROHIBITION:** לֹא (lo) = the absolute negative particle — "not." This is the apodictic negative (permanent, unconditional prohibition), as opposed to אַל (al), which is situational/temporary. יִהְיֶה (yihyeh) = qal imperfect 3ms of היה (hayah, "to be") — "there shall be" — the imperfect here has the force of a permanent decree: "there shall never be." לְךָ (lekha) = "for you, to you, belonging to you" — dative of possession. The prohibition is not merely against worshipping other gods but against having them at all — possessing them, acknowledging them, granting them any status in your life. אֱלֹהִים אֲחֵרִים (elohim acherim) = "other gods" — from אֱלֹהִים (elohim, "gods, God") + אֲחֵרִים (acherim, "other, different, strange," plural of אַחֵר, akher). The phrase does not explicitly deny the existence of other gods (that is a later theological development — explicit monotheism appears most clearly in Deutero-Isaiah, e.g., Isa 44:6–8; 45:5–7). It commands exclusive allegiance: whatever other divine beings may or may not exist, Yisra'el may not have them. עַל־פָּנָי (al-panai) = "upon/before my face" — from פָּנִים (panim, "face") + 1cs suffix. This phrase is debated: (1) "before me, in my presence" — i.e., in addition to me, alongside me; (2) "in preference to me, over me" — i.e., in my place; (3) "to my face, in defiance of me" — i.e., as an affront. The most literal reading is "upon my face" — the image of placing another god's image upon/over the face of YHWH, obscuring Him, replacing Him. Since YHWH is omnipresent, "before my face" means everywhere and always — there is no place or time where other gods are permissible. LXX: οὐκ ἔσονταί σοι θεοὶ ἕτεροι πλὴν ἐμοῦ ("there shall not be for you other gods besides me") — **LXX VARIANT:** πλὴν ἐμοῦ ("besides me, except me") for al-panai — the LXX interprets the spatial metaphor ("before my face") as an exclusionary statement ("besides me"), making the monotheistic implication more explicit.

**4** You shall not make [lo ta'aseh] for yourself [lekha] a carved image [pesel] or any [ve-khol] likeness [temunah] that [asher] [is] in the heavens [ba-shamayim] above [mi-ma'al], or that [va-asher] [is] in the earth [ba-arets] below [mi-takhat], or that [va-asher] [is] in the waters [ba-mayim] beneath [mi-takhat] the earth [la-arets].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא תַעֲשֶׂה־לְּךָ פֶסֶל וְכָל־תְּמוּנָה אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׁמַיִם מִמַּעַל וַאֲשֶׁר בָּאָרֶץ מִתָּחַת וַאֲשֶׁר בַּמַּיִם מִתַּחַת לָאָרֶץ (lo ta'aseh-lekha fesel ve-khol-temunah asher ba-shamayim mi-ma'al va-asher ba-arets mi-takhat va-asher ba-mayim mi-takhat la-arets, "you shall not make for yourself a carved image or any likeness that [is] in the heavens above, or that [is] in the earth below, or that [is] in the waters beneath the earth"). **Key terms:** לֹא תַעֲשֶׂה (lo ta'aseh) = "you shall not make" — qal imperfect 2ms of עשׂה (asah, "to do, make"). The prohibition is against making (manufacturing, crafting) — the act of creation of an image. פֶסֶל (fesel/pesel) = "carved image, idol, graven image" — from פסל (pasal, "to hew, carve, cut"). A pesel is an image carved from wood or stone, or cast from metal and then carved/finished. The word refers specifically to a sculpted, three-dimensional representation. וְכָל־תְּמוּנָה (ve-khol-temunah) = "or any likeness, or any form" — from תְּמוּנָה (temunah, "form, likeness, representation, image"). Temunah is broader than pesel: it encompasses any visual representation — carved, painted, drawn, or imagined. In Deut 4:12, Mosheh reminds the people: "You heard the sound of words but saw no temunah — only a voice" — at Sinai, God gave voice but no form. The prohibition against temunah is grounded in the Sinai experience itself: since you saw no form, you may make no form. The threefold cosmic inventory — שָׁמַיִם (shamayim, "heavens") / אֶרֶץ (erets, "earth") / מַיִם (mayim, "waters") — covers the entire ancient Near Eastern cosmology: the upper realm (sky, celestial bodies, birds), the middle realm (land, animals, humans), and the lower realm (subterranean waters, sea creatures, the deep). Nothing in any realm of existence may serve as a model for representing God. The three-tier cosmology mirrors the creation account of Genesis 1. LXX: οὐ ποιήσεις σεαυτῷ εἴδωλον οὐδὲ παντὸς ὁμοίωμα, ὅσα ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ ἄνω καὶ ὅσα ἐν τῇ γῇ κάτω καὶ ὅσα ἐν τοῖς ὕδασιν ὑποκάτω τῆς γῆς ("you shall not make for yourself an idol nor any likeness, of whatever is in the heaven above, or whatever is on the earth below, or whatever is in the waters beneath the earth") — εἴδωλον ("idol, image, phantom") for pesel — the Greek word eidolon (from which English "idol" derives) means "image, phantom, apparition" — something that looks like a reality but is not; ὁμοίωμα ("likeness, resemblance") for temunah.

**5** You shall not bow down [lo tishtakhaveh] to them [lahem] and you shall not serve them [ve-lo to'ovdem]; for I [ki anokhi] [am] YHWH your God [Elohekha], a jealous [qanna] God [El], visiting [poqed] the iniquity [avon] of [the] fathers [avot] upon [al] [the] sons [banim], upon the third [al-shileshim] and upon the fourth generation [ve-al-ribbe'im] of those who hate me [le-son'ai];

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא־תִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה לָהֶם וְלֹא תָעָבְדֵם כִּי אָנֹכִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ אֵל קַנָּא פֹּקֵד עֲוֹן אָבֹת עַל־בָּנִים עַל־שִׁלֵּשִׁים וְעַל־רִבֵּעִים לְשֹׂנְאָי (lo-tishtakhaveh lahem ve-lo to'ovdem ki anokhi YHWH Elohekha El qanna poqed avon avot al-banim al-shileshim ve-al-ribbe'im le-son'ai, "you shall not bow down to them and you shall not serve them; for I [am] YHWH your God, a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of fathers upon sons, upon the third and upon the fourth generation, of those who hate me"). **Key terms:** לֹא־תִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה (lo-tishtakhaveh) = hishtaphel (reflexive) of חוה (khavah/shakhah, "to bow down, prostrate oneself") — "you shall not bow down." The hishtaphel is an unusual conjugation found mainly with this root. Bowing down (hishtakhavah) is the physical act of prostration — face to the ground, body flat — the most extreme posture of submission and worship. The physical posture embodies total surrender. וְלֹא תָעָבְדֵם (ve-lo to'ovdem) = "and you shall not serve them" — qal imperfect 2ms of עבד (avad, "to serve, work, be a slave to") + 3mp suffix. The verb avad is the same root as עֶבֶד (eved, "slave/servant") and עֲבוֹדָה (avodah, "service/worship/labor"). To worship a god is to serve it — worship and servitude are the same word. Yisra'el was just freed from serving (avad) Pharaoh in the house of slavery (beit avadim); now they must not exchange that slavery for servitude to false gods. **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — EL QANNA:** אֵל קַנָּא (El qanna) = "a jealous/zealous/impassioned God" — from קנא (qana, "to be jealous, zealous, envious"). Qanna is an adjective form used exclusively of God (Ex 20:5; 34:14; Deut 4:24; 5:9; 6:15). The word describes an intense, exclusive passion — the passion of a spouse who will not share the beloved. God's "jealousy" is not petty envy but fierce, protective love that demands exclusivity. The marriage metaphor for the covenant is already implicit here (and becomes explicit in the prophets: Hos 1–3; Jer 2–3; Ezek 16; 23). פֹּקֵד (poqed) = qal active participle of פקד (paqad, "to visit, attend to, muster, appoint, reckon") — "visiting, attending to." Paqad is one of the most semantically rich verbs in Hebrew: it can mean to visit (for good or ill), to inspect, to take account of, to punish, to appoint. Here the "visiting" is punitive. עֲוֹן (avon) = "iniquity, guilt, punishment for guilt" — from עוה (avah, "to be crooked, bent, perverse"). Avon encompasses the sin, the guilt it produces, and the punishment it brings — all three dimensions in one word. עַל־שִׁלֵּשִׁים וְעַל־רִבֵּעִים (al-shileshim ve-al-ribbe'im) = "upon the third and upon the fourth generation" — from שִׁלֵּשׁ (shillesh, "third") and רִבֵּעַ (ribbea, "fourth"). The punishment extends three to four generations — the span of a living family in the ancient world (great-grandfather to great-grandchild could be alive simultaneously). The qualifier לְשֹׂנְאָי (le-son'ai) = "to/of those who hate me" (from שׂנא, sane, "to hate" + 1cs suffix) is crucial: the transgenerational consequence applies specifically to those who continue in the pattern of hatred toward God, not indiscriminately. Ezek 18:20 clarifies: "The son shall not bear the iniquity of the father." LXX: οὐ προσκυνήσεις αὐτοῖς οὐδὲ μὴ λατρεύσῃς αὐτοῖς· ἐγὼ γάρ εἰμι κύριος ὁ θεός σου, θεὸς ζηλωτής, ἀποδιδοὺς ἁμαρτίας πατέρων ἐπὶ τέκνα ἕως τρίτης καὶ τετάρτης γενεᾶς τοῖς μισοῦσίν με ("you shall not bow down to them nor serve them; for I am the Lord your God, a jealous God, repaying the sins of fathers upon children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me") — ζηλωτής ("zealot, jealous one") for qanna; ἀποδίδωμι ("give back, repay, recompense") for poqed — the LXX interprets "visiting" as "repaying."

**6** and doing [ve-oseh] loyal love [khesed] to thousands [la-alafim] of those who love me [le-ohavai] and of those who keep [u-le-shomrei] my commandments [mitsvotai].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעֹשֶׂה חֶסֶד לַאֲלָפִים לְאֹהֲבַי וּלְשֹׁמְרֵי מִצְוֹתָי (ve-oseh khesed la-alafim le-ohavai u-le-shomrei mitsvotai, "and doing loyal love to thousands, to those who love me and to those who keep my commandments"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE ASYMMETRY OF JUDGMENT AND MERCY:** The punishment extends to three or four generations (v. 5); the loyal love extends to thousands (alafim). The ratio is staggering: God's mercy outweighs His judgment by a factor of approximately 250 to 1 (or more — alafim could mean "thousands of generations"). This asymmetry is the beating heart of biblical theology: God is more inclined to bless than to punish. חֶסֶד (khesed) = "loyal love, steadfast love, covenant faithfulness, lovingkindness, mercy, grace" — one of the most important and most untranslatable words in the Hebrew Bible. Khesed is not mere emotion but covenantal commitment acted out in concrete faithfulness. It is the love that endures, the mercy that persists, the loyalty that refuses to let go. It is what God does because of who God is and because of the relationship He has chosen. לַאֲלָפִים (la-alafim) = "to/for thousands" — from אֶלֶף (elef, "thousand"). Whether "thousands [of generations]" or simply "thousands [of people]," the scope is vast beyond calculation. לְאֹהֲבַי (le-ohavai) = "to those who love me" — qal active participle of אהב (ahav, "to love") + 1cs suffix. The counterpart of "those who hate me" (v. 5). The covenant is framed in the language of love and hate — personal, relational, not merely legal. וּלְשֹׁמְרֵי מִצְוֹתָי (u-le-shomrei mitsvotai) = "and to those who keep my commandments" — from שׁמר (shamar, "to keep, guard, observe") + מִצְוָה (mitsvah, "commandment, charge," from צוה, tsavah, "to command") + 1cs suffix. Love and obedience are parallel, not opposed: to love God is to keep His commandments (cf. John 14:15). LXX: καὶ ποιῶν ἔλεος εἰς χιλιάδας τοῖς ἀγαπῶσίν με καὶ τοῖς φυλάσσουσιν τὰ προστάγματά μου ("and doing mercy to thousands, to those who love me and to those who keep my ordinances") — ἔλεος ("mercy, compassion, pity") for khesed; πρόσταγμα ("ordinance, command, decree") for mitsvotai.

**7** You shall not lift up [lo tissa] the name [et-shem] of YHWH your God [Elohekha] for emptiness [la-shav]; for YHWH will not hold guiltless [lo yenaqqeh] the one who lifts up [et-asher yissa] his name [et-shemo] for emptiness [la-shav].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא תִשָּׂא אֶת־שֵׁם יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לַשָּׁוְא כִּי לֹא יְנַקֶּה יְהוָה אֵת אֲשֶׁר יִשָּׂא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ לַשָּׁוְא (lo tissa et-shem YHWH Elohekha la-shav ki lo yenaqqeh YHWH et asher yissa et-shemo la-shav, "you shall not lift up the name of YHWH your God for emptiness, for YHWH will not hold guiltless the one who lifts up his name for emptiness"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE NAME AND ITS MISUSE:** לֹא תִשָּׂא (lo tissa) = "you shall not lift up, you shall not carry, you shall not bear" — qal imperfect 2ms of נשׂא (nasa, "to lift, carry, bear"). The verb nasa is strikingly physical: you shall not pick up, you shall not carry around, you shall not bear upon yourself. The name of God is not a tool to be picked up and deployed for human purposes. שֵׁם (shem) = "name" — in biblical thought, the name is not a mere label but the essence, character, and reputation of the person. To invoke God's name is to invoke God's presence, power, and authority. Misusing the name is misusing God Himself. לַשָּׁוְא (la-shav) = "for emptiness, for vanity, for nothing, for falsehood" — from שָׁוְא (shav, "emptiness, vanity, falsehood, worthlessness, deception"). Shav is broader than "vain" — it encompasses: (1) empty speech (invoking God's name in idle, meaningless talk); (2) false oaths (swearing by God's name to confirm a lie); (3) magical manipulation (using the divine name as a spell or incantation); (4) any use of God's name that empties it of its sacred weight. The traditional rendering "in vain" captures something of the meaning, but "for emptiness/falsehood" is more precise. The prohibition is not merely against casual swearing but against any deployment of the divine name that treats it as empty rather than full, as a human tool rather than a divine reality. לֹא יְנַקֶּה (lo yenaqqeh) = "he will not hold guiltless, he will not leave unpunished" — piel of נקה (naqah, "to be clean, innocent, acquitted"). The piel is declarative: YHWH will not declare clean/innocent the person who misuses His name. This is the only commandment in the Decalogue that includes its own enforcement clause within the commandment itself — as if to say: do not think this is a minor offense; God takes personal responsibility for punishing it. LXX: οὐ λήμψῃ τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ σου ἐπὶ ματαίῳ· οὐ γὰρ μὴ καθαρίσῃ κύριος τὸν λαμβάνοντα τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ ματαίῳ ("you shall not take the name of the Lord your God upon emptiness; for the Lord will absolutely not purify the one taking his name upon emptiness") — λαμβάνω ("take, receive") for tissa; μάταιος ("empty, futile, vain") for shav; καθαρίζω ("purify, cleanse") for yenaqqeh; the double negative οὐ μή intensifies the certainty of the punishment.

**8** Remember [zakhor] the day [et-yom] of the Shabbat [ha-shabbat] to make it holy [le-qaddesho].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads זָכוֹר אֶת־יוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת לְקַדְּשׁוֹ (zakhor et-yom ha-shabbat le-qaddesho, "remember the day of the Shabbat to make it holy"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — ZAKHOR vs. SHAMOR:** זָכוֹר (zakhor) = qal infinitive absolute of זכר (zakhar, "to remember, recall, bring to mind") — used as an imperative: "remember!" The infinitive absolute as imperative has a timeless, absolute quality — not "remember today" or "remember once" but "remember always." The parallel passage in Deuteronomy 5:12 reads שָׁמוֹר (shamor, "observe/guard/keep") instead of zakhor. The rabbinic tradition reconciles the two: "zakhor and shamor were spoken in a single utterance" (be-dibbur ekhad ne'emru) — the Friday evening liturgical hymn Lekha Dodi begins with this principle. Zakhor is the inward, cognitive dimension (remember, call to mind, hold in consciousness); shamor is the outward, active dimension (guard, observe, practice). The two together encompass the fullness of Shabbat observance. שַׁבָּת (shabbat) = "cessation, rest, Sabbath" — from שׁבת (shavat, "to cease, desist, rest"). The word is not primarily about rest in the sense of relaxation but about cessation — stopping, desisting from creative labor, withdrawing from the manipulation of the world. The Shabbat is sanctified time — the first thing declared holy in the Bible is not a place or a person but a day (Gen 2:3). לְקַדְּשׁוֹ (le-qaddesho) = "to make it holy, to sanctify it" — piel infinitive of קדשׁ (qadash, "to be holy, set apart") + 3ms suffix. The human act of keeping Shabbat participates in the divine act of sanctifying time. God made the day holy (Gen 2:3); Israel is commanded to make it holy in practice. LXX: μνήσθητι τὴν ἡμέραν τῶν σαββάτων ἁγιάζειν αὐτήν ("remember the day of the sabbaths to sanctify it") — μιμνήσκομαι ("remember, call to mind") for zakhor; σάββατον (sabbaton, a transliteration of the Hebrew rather than a translation) for shabbat; ἁγιάζω ("sanctify, make holy") for le-qaddesho. The LXX uses the plural τῶν σαββάτων ("of the sabbaths"), perhaps reflecting the recurring nature of the observance.

**9** Six [sheshet] days [yamim] you shall labor [ta'avod] and you shall do [ve-asita] all [kol] your work [melakhtekha];

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שֵׁשֶׁת יָמִים תַּעֲבֹד וְעָשִׂיתָ כָּל־מְלַאכְתֶּךָ (sheshet yamim ta'avod ve-asita kol-melakhtekha, "six days you shall labor and you shall do all your work"). **Key terms:** שֵׁשֶׁת יָמִים (sheshet yamim) = "six days" — the construct form. The six days of labor are not merely permitted but commanded — work is a divine mandate, not a curse. The rhythm is six-and-one, labor-and-rest, activity-and-cessation. תַּעֲבֹד (ta'avod) = qal imperfect 2ms of עבד (avad, "to work, serve, labor") — "you shall labor/work." The same root as עֶבֶד (eved, "slave") and עֲבוֹדָה (avodah, "work/service/worship"). Human labor during the six days is a form of service — it participates in God's creative work. מְלַאכְתֶּךָ (melakhtekha) = "your work" — from מְלָאכָה (melakhah, "work, craft, skilled labor, business") + 2ms suffix. Melakhah is the specific term the rabbis use for the categories of labor prohibited on Shabbat — they enumerate 39 categories (avot melakhah) derived from the types of work performed in building the mishkan (Tabernacle). The word is distinct from עֲבוֹדָה (avodah, general labor/service): melakhah implies purposeful, creative, world-shaping work. LXX: ἓξ ἡμέρας ἐργᾷ καὶ ποιήσεις πάντα τὰ ἔργα σου ("six days you shall work and do all your works") — ἐργάζομαι ("work, labor") for ta'avod; ἔργον ("work, deed") for melakhah.

**10** but the seventh [ha-shevi'i] day [ve-yom] [is] a Shabbat to YHWH your God [Elohekha]: you shall not do [lo ta'aseh] any [kol] work [melakhah] — you [attah], and your son [u-vinkha], and your daughter [u-vittekha], your male slave [avdekha] and your female slave [va-amatekha], and your beast [u-vehemtekha], and your sojourner [ve-gerekha] who [asher] [is] within your gates [bi-she'arekha].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְיוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי שַׁבָּת לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לֹא תַעֲשֶׂה כָל מְלָאכָה אַתָּה וּבִנְךָ וּבִתְּךָ עַבְדְּךָ וַאֲמָתְךָ וּבְהֶמְתֶּךָ וְגֵרְךָ אֲשֶׁר בִּשְׁעָרֶיךָ (ve-yom ha-shevi'i shabbat la-YHWH Elohekha lo ta'aseh khol melakhah attah u-vinkha u-vittekha avdekha va-amatekha u-vehemtekha ve-gerekha asher bi-she'arekha, "and the seventh day [is] a Shabbat to YHWH your God: you shall not do any work — you, and your son, and your daughter, your male slave and your female slave, and your beast, and your sojourner who [is] within your gates"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE RADICAL INCLUSIVENESS OF SHABBAT REST:** The list of those who must rest on Shabbat is a masterpiece of social legislation. It descends through the entire household hierarchy: אַתָּה (attah, "you" — the male head of household) → וּבִנְךָ (vinkha, "your son") → וּבִתְּךָ (vittekha, "your daughter") → עַבְדְּךָ (avdekha, "your male slave") → וַאֲמָתְךָ (amatekha, "your female slave") → וּבְהֶמְתֶּךָ (vehemtekha, "your beast, your animal") → וְגֵרְךָ (gerekha, "your sojourner, your resident alien"). Every member of the household, including those without power — slaves, animals, foreigners — must rest. The Shabbat is not a privilege of the free; it is a right extended to the enslaved. The God who liberated Yisra'el from slavery (v. 2) now mandates that even their slaves receive weekly liberation. גֵּר (ger) = "sojourner, resident alien, immigrant" — a non-Israelite living permanently within Israelite territory. The ger has no clan protection, no land inheritance, no social safety net — yet even the ger must rest on Shabbat. בִּשְׁעָרֶיךָ (bi-she'arekha) = "within your gates" — from שַׁעַר (sha'ar, "gate") + 2ms suffix. The "gates" represent the city/town boundary — the jurisdiction of the household head. LXX: τῇ δὲ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ σάββατα κυρίῳ τῷ θεῷ σου· οὐ ποιήσεις ἐν αὐτῇ πᾶν ἔργον, σὺ καὶ ὁ υἱός σου καὶ ἡ θυγάτηρ σου, ὁ παῖς σου καὶ ἡ παιδίσκη σου, ὁ βοῦς σου καὶ τὸ ὑποζύγιόν σου καὶ πᾶν κτῆνός σου, καὶ ὁ προσήλυτος ὁ παροικῶν ἐν σοί ("and on the seventh day is sabbaths to the Lord your God: you shall not do in it any work — you and your son and your daughter, your servant and your maidservant, your ox and your beast of burden and all your cattle, and the proselyte dwelling among you") — **LXX EXPANSION:** The LXX expands "your beast" into three categories: ὁ βοῦς σου ("your ox"), τὸ ὑποζύγιόν σου ("your beast of burden"), and πᾶν κτῆνός σου ("all your cattle"). προσήλυτος ("proselyte, convert") for ger — the LXX rendering shifts from "resident alien" to "religious convert," a significant theological interpretation.

**11** For [ki] [in] six [sheshet] days [yamim] YHWH made [asah] the heavens [et-ha-shamayim] and the earth [ve-et-ha-arets], the sea [et-ha-yam] and all [ve-et-kol] that [asher] [is] in them [bam], and he rested [vayanakh] on the seventh [ba-yom ha-shevi'i] day; therefore [al-ken] YHWH blessed [berekh] the day [et-yom] of the Shabbat [ha-shabbat] and he made it holy [vayeqaddeshehu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי שֵׁשֶׁת־יָמִים עָשָׂה יְהוָה אֶת־הַשָּׁמַיִם וְאֶת־הָאָרֶץ אֶת־הַיָּם וְאֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁר בָּם וַיָּנַח בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי עַל־כֵּן בֵּרַךְ יְהוָה אֶת־יוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת וַיְקַדְּשֵׁהוּ (ki sheshet-yamim asah YHWH et-ha-shamayim ve-et-ha-arets et-ha-yam ve-et-kol-asher bam vayanakh ba-yom ha-shevi'i al-ken berekh YHWH et-yom ha-shabbat vayeqaddeshehu, "for [in] six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth, the sea and all that [is] in them, and he rested on the seventh day; therefore YHWH blessed the day of the Shabbat and made it holy"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE CREATION MOTIVATION:** The Exodus version of the Shabbat commandment grounds it in creation (Gen 2:1–3); the Deuteronomy version (Deut 5:15) grounds it in liberation from Egypt. The two motivations are complementary: Shabbat is simultaneously a participation in divine creativity (resting as God rested after creating) and a participation in divine liberation (resting as the freed slave rests from Pharaoh's labor). וַיָּנַח (vayanakh) = qal of נוח (nuakh, "to rest, settle, be at rest") — "and he rested." This is a different verb from שׁבת (shavat, "to cease") used in Gen 2:2. Nuakh means to settle into rest, to find repose, to be at ease — it implies not just cessation of activity but positive enjoyment of tranquility. עַל־כֵּן (al-ken) = "therefore, on account of this, for this reason" — the Shabbat is not arbitrary; it is grounded in the structure of creation itself. The rhythm of work and rest is woven into the fabric of the universe. בֵּרַךְ (berekh) = piel of ברך (barakh, "to bless, kneel") — "he blessed." To bless is to endow with vitality, fertility, goodness, and divine favor. YHWH blessed the seventh day — He filled it with divine goodness, making it a source of life and renewal. וַיְקַדְּשֵׁהוּ (vayeqaddeshehu) = piel of קדשׁ (qadash) + 3ms suffix — "and he made it holy." God both blessed and sanctified the day — the double action of filling with goodness (blessing) and setting apart from the ordinary (sanctifying). LXX: ἐν γὰρ ἓξ ἡμέραις ἐποίησεν κύριος τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν καὶ τὴν θάλασσαν καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐν αὐτοῖς καὶ κατέπαυσεν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ἑβδόμῃ· διὰ τοῦτο εὐλόγησεν κύριος τὴν ἡμέραν τὴν ἑβδόμην καὶ ἡγίασεν αὐτήν ("for in six days the Lord made the heaven and the earth and the sea and all that is in them, and rested on the seventh day; therefore the Lord blessed the seventh day and sanctified it") — καταπαύω ("cause to rest, rest") for vayanakh; εὐλογέω ("bless, speak well of") for berekh.

## The Decalogue: Commands Toward Neighbor (vv. 12–17)

**12** Honor [kabbed] your father [et-avikha] and your mother [ve-et-immekha], in order that [lema'an] your days [yamekha] will be long [ya'arikhun] upon [al] the ground [ha-adamah] that YHWH your God [Elohekha] [is] giving [noten] to you [lakh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כַּבֵּד אֶת־אָבִיךָ וְאֶת־אִמֶּךָ לְמַעַן יַאֲרִכוּן יָמֶיךָ עַל הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ נֹתֵן לָךְ (kabbed et-avikha ve-et-immekha lema'an ya'arikhun yamekha al ha-adamah asher YHWH Elohekha noten lakh, "honor your father and your mother, in order that your days will be long upon the ground that YHWH your God is giving to you"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — KABBED AND THE BRIDGE COMMANDMENT:** כַּבֵּד (kabbed) = piel imperative of כבד (kaved, "to be heavy, weighty, honored, glorified") — "honor, give weight to, treat as heavy/significant." The root כבד is the same root as כָּבוֹד (kavod, "glory, weight, honor") and כָּבֵד (kaved, "heavy, weighty") — the adjective used to describe Pharaoh's heart ("heavy/hardened," Ex 7:14; 8:15; 9:7; 9:34; 10:1) and the cloud on Sinai (19:16, anan kaved, "heavy cloud"). To "honor" parents is to treat them as weighty, significant, glorious — the opposite of treating them as light (קלל, qalal, "to be light, to curse" — the antonym of kaved). This commandment stands at the hinge between the God-ward commandments (vv. 3–11) and the neighbor-ward commandments (vv. 13–17). Parents mediate between God and child — they are the first authority, the first face of God the child knows. To honor parents is simultaneously a duty toward humans and toward God. אֶת־אָבִיךָ וְאֶת־אִמֶּךָ (et-avikha ve-et-immekha) = "your father and your mother" — both parents receive the definite object marker אֵת, giving them equal grammatical weight. The mother is not subordinate to the father in this command. This is the only commandment with a promised reward: לְמַעַן יַאֲרִכוּן יָמֶיךָ (lema'an ya'arikhun yamekha) = "in order that your days will be long" — hiphil of אָרַךְ (arakh, "to be long, to prolong") + יָמִים (yamim, "days") — longevity upon the land. The connection between honoring parents and long life in the land suggests that social stability (respect for elders, maintenance of family structure) is the foundation of national survival. עַל הָאֲדָמָה (al ha-adamah) = "upon the ground/soil" — from אֲדָמָה (adamah, "ground, soil, land"), related to אָדָם (adam, "human"). The promised land is adamah — the same ground from which humanity was formed (Gen 2:7). LXX: τίμα τὸν πατέρα σου καὶ τὴν μητέρα σου, ἵνα εὖ σοι γένηται, καὶ ἵνα μακροχρόνιος γένῃ ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς τῆς ἀγαθῆς, ἧς κύριος ὁ θεός σου δίδωσίν σοι ("honor your father and your mother, that it may be well with you, and that you may be long-lived upon the good land that the Lord your God gives you") — **LXX EXPANSION:** The LXX adds ἵνα εὖ σοι γένηται ("that it may be well with you") and τῆς ἀγαθῆς ("the good [land]"), expansions not present in the MT Exodus version (though cf. Deut 5:16, which includes "that it may be well with you"). Paul quotes this commandment in Eph 6:2–3 as "the first commandment with a promise." τιμάω ("honor, value, prize") for kabbed.

**13** You shall not murder [lo tirtsakh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא תִּרְצָח (lo tirtsakh, "you shall not murder"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — RATSAKH vs. HARAG:** לֹא תִּרְצָח (lo tirtsakh) = qal imperfect 2ms of רצח (ratsakh, "to murder, slay") — "you shall not murder." The verb רצח (ratsakh) is specific and narrow: it refers to unlawful killing — murder, manslaughter, assassination. It is NOT the general word for killing, which is הרג (harag, "to kill, slay") or מות (mut, "to cause death") in the hiphil. The Torah elsewhere commands killing in various contexts: capital punishment (Ex 21:12–17), warfare (Deut 20), and self-defense. The verb ratsakh excludes these sanctioned forms of killing. The traditional rendering "Thou shalt not kill" (KJV) is too broad; "You shall not murder" is more accurate. Ratsakh appears 47 times in the OT, predominantly in legal contexts (the cities of refuge for the rotseakh, "killer/manslayer" — Num 35; Deut 4:41–43; 19:1–13; Josh 20). The commandment protects the sanctity of human life by prohibiting its unauthorized termination. LXX (Vaticanus): οὐ μοιχεύσεις ("you shall not commit adultery") — **LXX CRITICAL VARIANT:** Codex Vaticanus places the adultery prohibition here (v. 13) and the murder prohibition at v. 15, reversing the MT order. This alternative sequence (adultery — theft — murder) is also found in some NT quotations and in Philo. The standard LXX text (following the MT order) reads: οὐ φονεύσεις ("you shall not murder") — φονεύω ("murder, kill, commit homicide") for tirtsakh.

**14** You shall not commit adultery [lo tin'af].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא תִּנְאָף (lo tin'af, "you shall not commit adultery"). **Key terms:** לֹא תִּנְאָף (lo tin'af) = qal imperfect 2ms of נאף (na'af, "to commit adultery") — "you shall not commit adultery." The verb na'af in biblical law specifically refers to sexual intercourse between a man and another man's wife (or between a married/betrothed woman and a man who is not her husband). It is a violation of the marriage covenant — the exclusive sexual bond between husband and wife. In the broader prophetic tradition, na'af becomes a metaphor for Yisra'el's spiritual infidelity to YHWH (Jer 3:8–9; Ezek 16:32; Hos 2:4; 3:1) — the covenant between God and Yisra'el is a marriage, and idolatry is adultery. The commandment thus echoes and reinforces vv. 3–5: just as Yisra'el must not take other gods, so the married person must not take other partners. The divine-human covenant and the human-human marriage covenant mirror each other. LXX (Vaticanus): οὐ κλέψεις ("you shall not steal") — following the Vaticanus order. Standard LXX: οὐ μοιχεύσεις ("you shall not commit adultery") — μοιχεύω ("commit adultery") for tin'af.

**15** You shall not steal [lo tignov].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא תִּגְנֹב (lo tignov, "you shall not steal"). **Key terms:** לֹא תִּגְנֹב (lo tignov) = qal imperfect 2ms of גנב (ganav, "to steal, take by stealth") — "you shall not steal." The verb ganav means to take another's property secretly, by stealth (as opposed to גזל, gazal, "to rob, seize by force/violence"). The ganav ("thief") operates by deception and concealment; the gazlan ("robber") operates by open force. The Mekhilta and Talmud (Sanhedrin 86a) interpret this commandment as referring specifically to kidnapping — stealing a person (since the penalty context of the Decalogue implies capital crimes, and the theft of property is addressed separately in Ex 21:37–22:3 with non-capital penalties). If this interpretation is correct, the three short commandments (vv. 13–15) protect life, marriage, and personal freedom respectively — the three most fundamental human rights. LXX (Vaticanus): οὐ φονεύσεις ("you shall not murder") — following the Vaticanus order. Standard LXX: οὐ κλέψεις ("you shall not steal") — κλέπτω ("steal") for tignov.

**16** You shall not answer [lo ta'aneh] against your neighbor [ve-re'akha] [as] a witness [ed] of falsehood [shaqer].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא תַעֲנֶה בְרֵעֲךָ עֵד שָׁקֶר (lo ta'aneh ve-re'akha ed shaqer, "you shall not answer against your neighbor [as] a witness of falsehood"). **Key terms:** לֹא תַעֲנֶה (lo ta'aneh) = qal imperfect 2ms of ענה (anah, "to answer, respond, testify") — "you shall not answer/testify." The verb anah in legal contexts means to give testimony, to respond to questioning in a judicial proceeding. בְרֵעֲךָ (ve-re'akha) = "against/concerning your neighbor" — from רֵעַ (rea, "neighbor, companion, friend, fellow") + 2ms suffix. The preposition בְּ (be) here means "against, concerning" — testimony given regarding your neighbor. רֵעַ (rea) is broader than "neighbor" in the modern sense: it means any fellow member of the community, any person with whom you have dealings. עֵד שָׁקֶר (ed shaqer) = "a witness of falsehood / a false witness" — from עֵד (ed, "witness, testimony") + שֶׁקֶר (sheqer, "falsehood, deception, lie"). Sheqer is distinct from שָׁוְא (shav, "emptiness/vanity," used in v. 7): sheqer is active, deliberate deception — a calculated lie. The Deuteronomy parallel (5:20) reads עֵד שָׁוְא (ed shav, "a witness of emptiness/vanity") rather than ed sheqer. The difference: shav suggests testimony that is groundless, empty, without substance; sheqer suggests testimony that is intentionally false, fabricated. The commandment protects the integrity of the judicial system — without truthful testimony, justice collapses and the innocent suffer. LXX: οὐ ψευδομαρτυρήσεις κατὰ τοῦ πλησίον σου μαρτυρίαν ψευδῆ ("you shall not bear false witness against your neighbor, a false testimony") — ψευδομαρτυρέω ("bear false witness," a compound verb: ψεῦδος + μαρτυρέω) for ta'aneh ed shaqer; πλησίον ("neighbor, one nearby") for rea.

**17** You shall not covet [lo takhmod] the house [beit] of your neighbor [re'ekha]; you shall not covet [lo takhmod] the wife [eshet] of your neighbor [re'ekha], or his male slave [ve-avdo], or his female slave [va-amato], or his ox [ve-shoro], or his donkey [va-khamoro], or anything [ve-khol] that [asher] [belongs] to your neighbor [le-re'ekha].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא תַחְמֹד בֵּית רֵעֶךָ לֹא תַחְמֹד אֵשֶׁת רֵעֶךָ וְעַבְדּוֹ וַאֲמָתוֹ וְשׁוֹרוֹ וַחֲמֹרוֹ וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר לְרֵעֶךָ (lo takhmod beit re'ekha lo takhmod eshet re'ekha ve-avdo va-amato ve-shoro va-khamoro ve-khol asher le-re'ekha, "you shall not covet the house of your neighbor; you shall not covet the wife of your neighbor, or his male slave, or his female slave, or his ox, or his donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE INTERIOR COMMANDMENT:** לֹא תַחְמֹד (lo takhmod) = qal imperfect 2ms of חמד (khamad, "to covet, desire, delight in, take pleasure in") — "you shall not covet/desire." The verb khamad describes an intense, consuming desire for something that belongs to another — not mere appreciation ("that's a nice house") but acquisitive longing ("I want that house to be mine"). This commandment is unique among the ten because it legislates the internal state — not an action (murder, theft, adultery) but an emotion, a desire, a thought. All the preceding commandments govern behavior; this one governs the heart. It implies that external obedience without internal transformation is insufficient — the Torah demands not just right action but right desire. The Deuteronomy parallel (5:21) uses two different verbs: לֹא תַחְמֹד (lo takhmod, "you shall not covet") for the wife, and לֹא תִתְאַוֶּה (lo tit'avveh, "you shall not desire/crave") for the house and possessions — distinguishing between khamad (desire that leads to action/acquisition) and avah (raw desire/craving). The Catholic/Lutheran tradition splits this verse into two commandments (coveting wife / coveting goods), yielding ten by combining the image prohibition with the first commandment. **The list of prohibited objects of desire:** בֵּית (bayit, "house") — dwelling, household, estate; אֵשֶׁת (eshet, "wife of") — the wife listed among the household, reflecting the patriarchal social structure where a wife was part of the household under the husband's authority; עֶבֶד (eved, "male slave"); אָמָה (amah, "female slave"); שׁוֹר (shor, "ox") — the primary work animal; חֲמוֹר (khamor, "donkey") — the primary transport animal; וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר לְרֵעֶךָ (ve-khol asher le-re'ekha, "and all that belongs to your neighbor") — the comprehensive catch-all, ensuring that no form of covetousness escapes. LXX: οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις τὴν γυναῖκα τοῦ πλησίον σου· οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις τὴν οἰκίαν τοῦ πλησίον σου οὔτε τὸν ἀγρὸν αὐτοῦ οὔτε τὸν παῖδα αὐτοῦ οὔτε τὴν παιδίσκην αὐτοῦ οὔτε τοῦ βοὸς αὐτοῦ οὔτε τοῦ ὑποζυγίου αὐτοῦ οὔτε παντὸς κτήνους αὐτοῦ οὔτε ὅσα τῷ πλησίον σού ἐστιν ("you shall not desire the wife of your neighbor; you shall not desire the house of your neighbor, nor his field, nor his servant, nor his maidservant, nor his ox, nor his beast of burden, nor any of his cattle, nor whatever belongs to your neighbor") — **LXX CRITICAL VARIANT:** The LXX reverses the order, placing the wife before the house (separating them into two distinct prohibitions), and adds τὸν ἀγρόν ("his field"), not present in the MT Exodus version (though present in Deut 5:21). ἐπιθυμέω ("desire, covet, long for") for takhmod.

## The People's Terror and Retreat (vv. 18–21)

**18** And all the people [ve-khol-ha-am] [were] seeing [ro'im] the thunders [et-ha-qolot] and the torches [ve-et-ha-lappidim] and the sound [ve-et qol] of the shofar [ha-shofar] and the mountain [ve-et-ha-har] smoking [ashen]; and the people saw [vayar ha-am] and they trembled [vayanu'u] and they stood [vaya'amdu] at a distance [me-rakhoq].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכָל־הָעָם רֹאִים אֶת־הַקּוֹלֹת וְאֶת־הַלַּפִּידִם וְאֵת קוֹל הַשֹּׁפָר וְאֶת־הָהָר עָשֵׁן וַיַּרְא הָעָם וַיָּנֻעוּ וַיַּעַמְדוּ מֵרָחֹק (ve-khol-ha-am ro'im et-ha-qolot ve-et-ha-lappidim ve-et qol ha-shofar ve-et-ha-har ashen vayar ha-am vayanu'u vaya'amdu me-rakhoq, "and all the people were seeing the thunders and the torches and the sound of the shofar and the mountain smoking; and the people saw and they trembled and they stood at a distance"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — SEEING SOUNDS:** The verse contains a famous synesthesia: the people are רֹאִים אֶת־הַקּוֹלֹת (ro'im et-ha-qolot) — "seeing the voices/thunders." They see what should only be heard. The Mekhilta comments: "They saw what is [normally] heard, and they heard what is [normally] seen" — at Sinai, the boundaries between the senses collapsed. The theophany overwhelmed normal perceptual categories. רֹאִים (ro'im) = qal active participle plural of ראה (ra'ah, "to see") — "seeing." The participle indicates ongoing, continuous perception — not a momentary glimpse but sustained visual experience. הַלַּפִּידִם (ha-lappidim) = "the torches, the flames" — plural of לַפִּיד (lappid, "torch, flame, flash"). These are not the beraqim ("lightnings") of 19:16 but lappidim — torches, fire-flashes, tongues of flame. The image recalls Gen 15:17, where a "torch of fire" (lappid esh) passed between the pieces of Avraham's covenant sacrifice. וַיָּנֻעוּ (vayanu'u) = qal of נוע (nua, "to tremble, shake, stagger, waver, wander") — "and they trembled/staggered." The verb nua suggests unsteady, tottering movement — the people literally reeled, staggered back on their feet, as if physically struck by the force of the theophany. מֵרָחֹק (me-rakhoq) = "at a distance, from far off" — from רָחוֹק (rakhoq, "far, distant"). The people retreat to a safe distance. The impulse to draw near (which YHWH warned against in 19:21) has been replaced by the impulse to flee. LXX: καὶ πᾶς ὁ λαὸς ἑώρα τὴν φωνὴν καὶ τὰς λαμπάδας καὶ τὴν φωνὴν τῆς σάλπιγγος καὶ τὸ ὄρος τὸ καπνίζον· φοβηθέντες δὲ πᾶς ὁ λαὸς ἔστησαν μακρόθεν ("and all the people were seeing the voice and the torches and the sound of the trumpet and the mountain smoking; and all the people, being afraid, stood at a distance") — the LXX preserves the synesthetic "seeing the voice" (ἑώρα τὴν φωνήν); λαμπάς ("torch, lamp") for lappid; φοβέω ("fear, be afraid") replaces the more physical vayanu'u ("they staggered").

**19** And they said [vayomeru] to Mosheh: "You speak [dabber attah] with us [immanu] and we will hear [ve-nishma'ah]; but let not God [ve-al-yedabber immanu] speak [Elohim] with us, lest [pen] we die [namut]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל־מֹשֶׁה דַּבֵּר אַתָּה עִמָּנוּ וְנִשְׁמָעָה וְאַל־יְדַבֵּר עִמָּנוּ אֱלֹהִים פֶּן־נָמוּת (vayomeru el-Mosheh dabber attah immanu ve-nishma'ah ve-al-yedabber immanu Elohim pen-namut, "and they said to Mosheh: 'You speak with us and we will hear; but let not God speak with us, lest we die'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE REQUEST FOR MEDIATION:** This verse marks a decisive turning point in the structure of biblical revelation. The people, having directly experienced the voice of God, request that Mosheh serve as intermediary. They cannot endure the unmediated presence of God — the experience is too overwhelming, too terrifying, too annihilating. דַּבֵּר אַתָּה (dabber attah) = "you speak" — piel imperative of דבר (davar, "to speak") + the emphatic independent pronoun אַתָּה (attah, "you"). The emphasis falls on "you" — YOU speak, not God. The people want human mediation: Mosheh's voice, not God's voice. וְנִשְׁמָעָה (ve-nishma'ah) = "and we will hear/obey" — qal cohortative 1cp of שׁמע (shama, "to hear, obey"). The cohortative (-ah ending) expresses willingness: "and we are willing to hear, and let us hear." They are not refusing obedience — they are requesting a change in the mode of communication. וְאַל־יְדַבֵּר עִמָּנוּ אֱלֹהִים (ve-al-yedabber immanu Elohim) = "and let not God speak with us" — the jussive negative (al + imperfect) expresses a wish or plea. פֶּן־נָמוּת (pen-namut) = "lest we die" — from מות (mut, "to die"). The people genuinely believe they will die if God continues to speak directly. The tradition affirms this: Deut 5:24–27 expands this plea and records YHWH's remarkable response: "They have spoken well" (hetavu asher dibberu) — God approves the request for mediation. The establishment of prophecy as an institution flows from this moment: the people's terror at Sinai creates the need for prophets. LXX: καὶ εἶπαν πρὸς Μωυσῆν· λάλησον σὺ ἡμῖν, καὶ μὴ λαλείτω πρὸς ἡμᾶς ὁ θεός, μήποτε ἀποθάνωμεν ("and they said to Mosheh: 'You speak to us, and let not God speak to us, lest we die'") — λαλέω ("speak, talk") for dabber; μήποτε ("lest, never") for pen.

**20** And Mosheh said to the people: "Do not fear [al-tira'u]; for in order to test you [le-nassot etkhem] ha-Elohim [God] has come [ba], and in order that [u-va-avur] his fear [yir'ato] will be [tihyeh] upon [al] your faces [peneikhem], so that [le-vilti] you will not sin [tekheta'u]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם אַל־תִּירָאוּ כִּי לְבַעֲבוּר נַסּוֹת אֶתְכֶם בָּא הָאֱלֹהִים וּבַעֲבוּר תִּהְיֶה יִרְאָתוֹ עַל־פְּנֵיכֶם לְבִלְתִּי תֶחֱטָאוּ (vayomer Mosheh el-ha-am al-tira'u ki le-va'avur nassot etkhem ba ha-Elohim u-va'avur tihyeh yir'ato al-peneikhem le-vilti tekheta'u, "and Mosheh said to the people: 'Do not fear; for in order to test you God has come, and in order that his fear will be upon your faces, so that you will not sin'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — FEAR AND THE FEAR OF GOD:** Mosheh's response contains a paradox: "Do not fear... so that his fear will be upon your faces." The first "fear" (תִּירָאוּ, tira'u) is the terror of annihilation — the panic that drives the people to flee. The second "fear" (יִרְאָה, yir'ah) is the reverential awe that restrains from sin — the healthy awareness of God's holiness that shapes moral behavior. Mosheh says: stop panicking, but do not lose the awe. The terror should transform into reverence. אַל־תִּירָאוּ (al-tira'u) = "do not fear" — qal imperfect 2mp of ירא (yare, "to fear, be afraid"). לְבַעֲבוּר נַסּוֹת (le-va'avur nassot) = "in order to test" — from נסה (nasah, "to test, try, prove"). The piel infinitive nassot indicates intentional testing — God's purpose in the theophany is to prove/test the people. The test is not to find out what they will do (God already knows) but to reveal to them their own capacity and their own limits. בָּא (ba) = qal perfect 3ms of בוא (bo, "to come") — "has come." God's coming is presented as an accomplished fact. יִרְאָתוֹ (yir'ato) = "his fear, the fear of him" — from יִרְאָה (yir'ah, "fear, awe, reverence") + 3ms suffix. Yir'at YHWH (the fear of YHWH) is the beginning of wisdom (Prov 1:7; 9:10; Job 28:28; Ps 111:10) — it is the foundational orientation toward God that makes all moral and intellectual life possible. עַל־פְּנֵיכֶם (al-peneikhem) = "upon your faces" — from פָּנִים (panim, "faces") + 2mp suffix. The fear of God is to be visible on their faces — not hidden in the heart but written on the countenance, shaping every expression and every glance. לְבִלְתִּי תֶחֱטָאוּ (le-vilti tekheta'u) = "so that you will not sin" — from חטא (khata, "to miss [the mark], sin, go wrong"). The ultimate purpose of the theophany is ethical: that the people should not sin. The awesome display of divine power serves a moral end — not to terrify for terror's sake but to produce the reverence that restrains from wrongdoing. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Μωυσῆς· θαρσεῖτε· ἕνεκεν γὰρ τοῦ πειράσαι ὑμᾶς παρεγένετο ὁ θεὸς πρὸς ὑμᾶς, ὅπως ἂν γένηται ὁ φόβος αὐτοῦ ἐν ὑμῖν, ἵνα μὴ ἁμαρτάνητε ("and Mosheh said to them: 'Take courage; for God has come to you in order to test you, so that his fear may be among you, that you may not sin'") — θαρσέω ("take courage, be of good cheer") for al-tira'u — a more positive rendering than "do not fear"; πειράζω ("test, try, tempt") for nassot; φόβος ("fear, terror, reverence") for yir'ah.

**21** And the people stood [vaya'amod ha-am] at a distance [me-rakhoq], and Mosheh drew near [niggash] to the thick darkness [el-ha-arafel] where [asher] [sham] ha-Elohim [God] [was].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּעֲמֹד הָעָם מֵרָחֹק וּמֹשֶׁה נִגַּשׁ אֶל־הָעֲרָפֶל אֲשֶׁר־שָׁם הָאֱלֹהִים (vaya'amod ha-am me-rakhoq u-Mosheh niggash el-ha-arafel asher-sham ha-Elohim, "and the people stood at a distance, and Mosheh drew near to the thick darkness where God [was]"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — THE ARAFEL AND THE PARADOX OF DIVINE DARKNESS:** This verse presents one of the most profound images in the Hebrew Bible: Mosheh walks into the darkness where God is, while the people stand far off. The contrast is absolute: the people retreat; Mosheh advances. The people seek distance; Mosheh seeks proximity. וַיַּעֲמֹד (vaya'amod) = qal of עמד (amad, "to stand, take one's stand, remain") — "and he/they stood." The people take up a fixed position — they will go no further. מֵרָחֹק (me-rakhoq) = "at a distance" — the same phrase as v. 18. Their retreat is confirmed as their settled position. נִגַּשׁ (niggash) = niphal of נגשׁ (nagash, "to draw near, approach") — "he drew near." The niphal here may have a reflexive sense: he approached of his own accord, he brought himself near. The verb nagash is the verb of priestly approach — the priest nagash to the altar, to the holy. Mosheh functions as the ultimate priest, the mediator par excellence. **THE ARAFEL:** עֲרָפֶל (arafel) = "thick darkness, deep gloom, dense cloud, impenetrable darkness" — a word that appears 15 times in the OT, often in theophanic contexts (Deut 4:11; 5:22; 2 Sam 22:10; 1 Kgs 8:12; Ps 18:10; 97:2). The arafel is not the absence of God but the intensified presence of God — God dwells in the arafel (1 Kgs 8:12: "YHWH has said he would dwell in the arafel"). The darkness is not ignorance but transcendence — God is so overwhelmingly present that human perception registers it as darkness, the way one is "blinded" by looking directly at the sun. The mystical tradition (both Jewish and Christian) has drawn deeply from this image: the "cloud of unknowing," the via negativa, the "luminous darkness" (Gregory of Nyssa) — the idea that the deepest knowledge of God is found not in conceptual clarity but in entering the darkness of divine transcendence. אֲשֶׁר־שָׁם הָאֱלֹהִים (asher-sham ha-Elohim) = "where God [was]" — literally "which there [was] God." The relative clause locates God spatially: God is in the thick darkness. The simple declaration is breathtaking — the arafel is the address of God. LXX: εἱστήκει δὲ ὁ λαὸς μακρόθεν, Μωυσῆς δὲ εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸν γνόφον, οὗ ἦν ὁ θεός ("and the people stood at a distance, but Mosheh entered into the deep darkness where God was") — γνόφος ("darkness, gloom, murk") for arafel; εἰσέρχομαι ("enter into, go into") for niggash — the LXX intensifies the image: Mosheh does not merely "draw near" but "enters into" the darkness.

## The Altar Law (vv. 22–26)

**22** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Thus [koh] you shall say [tomar] to the sons [el-benei] of Yisra'el: 'You yourselves [attem] have seen [re'item] that from the heavens [min-ha-shamayim] I have spoken [dibbarti] with you [immakhem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה כֹּה תֹאמַר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אַתֶּם רְאִיתֶם כִּי מִן־הַשָּׁמַיִם דִּבַּרְתִּי עִמָּכֶם (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh koh tomar el-benei Yisra'el attem re'item ki min-ha-shamayim dibbarti immakhem, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Thus you shall say to the sons of Yisra'el: You yourselves have seen that from the heavens I have spoken with you'"). **Key terms:** מִן־הַשָּׁמַיִם דִּבַּרְתִּי (min-ha-shamayim dibbarti) = "from the heavens I have spoken" — this seems to stand in tension with 19:20, which says YHWH descended upon Mount Sinai. Did God speak from heaven or from the mountain? The rabbis resolved this: "He bent the lower heavens and the upper heavens down upon the top of the mountain and spoke" (Mekhilta) — or, God's voice originated in heaven but was heard on the mountain. The theological point is that the speaker is the God of heaven, not a local mountain deity. אַתֶּם רְאִיתֶם (attem re'item) = "you yourselves have seen" — the same emphatic construction as 19:4. Again, God appeals to the people's direct experience. The emphasis on eyewitness experience is foundational to the Sinai tradition. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· τάδε ἐρεῖς τῷ οἴκῳ Ιακωβ καὶ ἀναγγελεῖς τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ· ὑμεῖς ἑωράκατε ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ λελάληκα πρὸς ὑμᾶς ("and the Lord said to Mosheh: 'These things you shall say to the house of Iakob and announce to the sons of Israel: You yourselves have seen that from heaven I have spoken to you'") — **LXX EXPANSION:** The LXX adds the formula "to the house of Iakob and announce to the sons of Israel" from 19:3, which is not in the MT here.

**23** You shall not make [lo ta'asun] with me [itti] — gods of [elohei] silver [khesef] and gods of [velohei] gold [zahav] you shall not make [lo ta'asu] for yourselves [lakhem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֹא תַעֲשׂוּן אִתִּי אֱלֹהֵי כֶסֶף וֵאלֹהֵי זָהָב לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ לָכֶם (lo ta'asun itti elohei khesef velohei zahav lo ta'asu lakhem, "you shall not make with me — gods of silver and gods of gold you shall not make for yourselves"). **Key terms:** לֹא תַעֲשׂוּן אִתִּי (lo ta'asun itti) = "you shall not make with me" — this difficult phrase has been interpreted several ways: (1) "you shall not make [anything to be] with me" — i.e., do not make any image to stand alongside me; (2) "you shall not make [an image] of me" — itti understood as a reflexive: do not make me in silver or gold; (3) "you shall not make [gods to be put] beside me." The most likely meaning combines (1) and (2): do not make representations of me, and do not make other gods to place beside me. The shift from singular "you" (in the Decalogue) to plural "you" (תַעֲשׂוּן, ta'asun) signals a transition from individual address to communal address — the altar law is directed to the community as a whole. אֱלֹהֵי כֶסֶף (elohei khesef) = "gods of silver" — from כֶּסֶף (kesef, "silver, money"). וֵאלֹהֵי זָהָב (velohei zahav) = "and gods of gold" — from זָהָב (zahav, "gold"). Silver and gold are the precious metals of the ancient world; the prohibition specifically targets luxury idols made from the most valued materials. The golden calf of Exodus 32 will violate precisely this prohibition. LXX: οὐ ποιήσετε ἑαυτοῖς θεοὺς ἀργυροῦς καὶ θεοὺς χρυσοῦς οὐ ποιήσετε ὑμῖν αὐτοῖς ("you shall not make for yourselves gods of silver, and gods of gold you shall not make for yourselves") — ἀργυροῦς ("of silver, silver") for kesef; χρυσοῦς ("of gold, golden") for zahav.

**24** An altar [mizbeakh] of earth [adamah] you shall make [ta'aseh] for me [li], and you shall sacrifice [ve-zavakhta] upon it [alav] your burnt offerings [et-olotekha] and your peace offerings [ve-et-shelamekha], your flock [et-tsonekha] and your herd [ve-et-beqarekha]; in every [be-khol] place [ha-maqom] where [asher] I cause my name to be remembered [azkir et-shemi], I will come [avo] to you and I will bless you [u-verakhttikha].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads מִזְבַּח אֲדָמָה תַּעֲשֶׂה־לִּי וְזָבַחְתָּ עָלָיו אֶת־עֹלֹתֶיךָ וְאֶת־שְׁלָמֶיךָ אֶת־צֹאנְךָ וְאֶת־בְּקָרֶךָ בְּכָל־הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַזְכִּיר אֶת־שְׁמִי אָבוֹא אֵלֶיךָ וּבֵרַכְתִּיךָ (mizbeakh adamah ta'aseh-li ve-zavakhta alav et-olotekha ve-et-shelamekha et-tsonekha ve-et-beqarekha be-khol-ha-maqom asher azkir et-shemi avo elekha u-verakhttikha, "an altar of earth you shall make for me, and you shall sacrifice upon it your burnt offerings and your peace offerings, your flock and your herd; in every place where I cause my name to be remembered, I will come to you and I will bless you"). **Key terms:** מִזְבַּח (mizbeakh) = "altar" — from זבח (zavakh, "to sacrifice, slaughter") — literally "a place of slaughtering/sacrificing." The altar is the focal point of worship, the place where heaven and earth meet through the medium of sacrifice. אֲדָמָה (adamah) = "earth, soil, ground" — the simplest possible material. The first altar is not to be made of precious metals or carved stone but of plain earth — the humblest substance, the substance from which humanity was formed (Gen 2:7). This stands in deliberate contrast to the silver and gold gods of v. 23. עֹלָה (olah, "burnt offering") = from עלה (alah, "to go up") — the offering that "goes up" entirely in smoke to God; nothing is eaten by the worshipper. שְׁלָמִים (shelamim, "peace offerings") = from שׁלם (shalam, "to be complete, whole, at peace") — the fellowship offering, shared between God (the fat portions burned), the priest, and the worshipper in a communal meal. צֹאן (tson, "flock" — sheep and goats) and בָּקָר (baqar, "herd" — cattle). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — "IN EVERY PLACE WHERE I CAUSE MY NAME TO BE REMEMBERED":** בְּכָל־הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַזְכִּיר אֶת־שְׁמִי (be-khol-ha-maqom asher azkir et-shemi) = "in every place where I cause my name to be remembered" — אַזְכִּיר (azkir) = hiphil of זכר (zakhar, "to remember") — "I cause to be remembered, I make mention of." God chooses the places of worship; humans do not. The divine initiative in worship mirrors the divine initiative in the covenant (v. 2). This verse appears to permit multiple places of worship (be-khol ha-maqom, "in every place"), which stands in tension with Deuteronomy's centralization of worship at "the place which YHWH will choose" (Deut 12:5–14). The altar law of Exodus 20 reflects an earlier, pre-centralization practice. אָבוֹא אֵלֶיךָ (avo elekha) = "I will come to you" — the divine promise of presence at the place of worship. וּבֵרַכְתִּיךָ (u-verakhttikha) = "and I will bless you" — piel of ברך (barakh, "to bless") + 2ms suffix. Worship and blessing are inseparable: where the name is invoked, God comes; where God comes, blessing follows. LXX: θυσιαστήριον ἐκ γῆς ποιήσετέ μοι καὶ θύσετε ἐπ᾽ αὐτοῦ τὰ ὁλοκαυτώματα ὑμῶν καὶ τὰ σωτήρια ὑμῶν, τὰ πρόβατα καὶ τοὺς μόσχους ὑμῶν, ἐν παντὶ τόπῳ οὗ ἐὰν ἐπονομάσω τὸ ὄνομά μου ἐκεῖ, καὶ ἥξω πρὸς σὲ καὶ εὐλογήσω σε ("an altar of earth you shall make for me, and you shall sacrifice upon it your burnt offerings and your salvation-offerings, your sheep and your calves, in every place where I name my name there, and I will come to you and bless you") — θυσιαστήριον ("altar, place of sacrifice") for mizbeakh; ὁλοκαύτωμα ("whole-burnt-offering") for olah; σωτήριον ("salvation-offering, peace-offering") for shelamim.

**25** And if [ve-im] an altar [mizbeakh] of stones [avanim] you make [ta'aseh] for me [li], you shall not build [lo tivneh] them [ethen] [as] hewn [gazit]; for [ki] your sword [kharbekha] you have swung [henafta] over it [alekha] and you have profaned it [va-tekhalelekha].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם־מִזְבַּח אֲבָנִים תַּעֲשֶׂה־לִּי לֹא תִבְנֶה אֶתְהֶן גָּזִית כִּי חַרְבְּךָ הֵנַפְתָּ עָלֶיהָ וַתְּחַלְלֶהָ (ve-im-mizbeakh avanim ta'aseh-li lo tivneh ethen gazit ki kharbekha henafta alekha va-tekhalelekha, "and if an altar of stones you make for me, you shall not build them [as] hewn [stone]; for your sword you have swung over it, and you have profaned it"). **Key terms:** אֲבָנִים (avanim) = "stones" — plural of אֶבֶן (even, "stone"). If the worshipper uses stones instead of earth, the stones must be uncut, in their natural state. גָּזִית (gazit) = "hewn stone, dressed stone, cut stone" — from גזה (gazah, "to cut, hew"). Gazit is stone that has been shaped by human tools — cut, squared, dressed for construction. The altar must not be built of dressed stone. חַרְבְּךָ (kharbekha) = "your sword" — from חֶרֶב (kherev, "sword, knife, cutting tool"). The kherev here is probably a chisel or cutting implement, not a battle sword. Any metal tool applied to the stone profanes it. הֵנַפְתָּ (henafta) = hiphil of נוף (nuf, "to swing, wave, brandish") — "you have swung, you have wielded." The act of swinging a metal tool over the stone desecrates it. וַתְּחַלְלֶהָ (va-tekhalelekha) = piel of חלל (khalal, "to profane, defile, desecrate, pollute") — "and you have profaned it." The root khalal is the opposite of קדשׁ (qadash, "to make holy"): to profane is to remove holiness, to turn the sacred into the common. The logic: the altar must be natural, untouched by human technology. Human craftsmanship, which shapes and masters materials, is inappropriate for the place where God and human meet. The altar represents surrender, not mastery. The same principle governs the altar of the Temple: "whole stones upon which no iron [tool] was lifted" (1 Kgs 6:7). LXX: ἐὰν δὲ θυσιαστήριον ἐκ λίθων ποιῇς μοι, οὐκ οἰκοδομήσεις αὐτοὺς τμητούς· τὸ γὰρ ἐγχειρίδιόν σου ἐπιβέβληκας ἐπ᾽ αὐτοὺς καὶ μεμίανται ("and if you make for me an altar of stones, you shall not build them hewn; for you have cast your tool upon them, and they have been defiled") — τμητός ("cut, hewn") for gazit; ἐγχειρίδιον ("hand-tool, dagger, chisel") for kherev — the LXX interprets kherev as a hand-tool/chisel rather than a sword; μιαίνω ("defile, pollute") for tekhalelekha.

**26** And you shall not go up [ve-lo ta'aleh] by steps [ve-ma'alot] upon [al] my altar [mizbekhi], so that [asher] your nakedness [ervatekha] will not be exposed [lo tigaleh] upon it [alav].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְלֹא תַעֲלֶה בְמַעֲלֹת עַל־מִזְבְּחִי אֲשֶׁר לֹא תִגָּלֶה עֶרְוָתְךָ עָלָיו (ve-lo ta'aleh ve-ma'alot al-mizbekhi asher lo tigaleh ervatekha alav, "and you shall not go up by steps upon my altar, so that your nakedness will not be exposed upon it"). **Key terms:** בְמַעֲלֹת (ve-ma'alot) = "by steps, by ascents" — from מַעֲלָה (ma'alah, "step, ascent, stairway"), from the root עלה (alah, "to go up"). The altar must not have steps — only a ramp (as the later Tabernacle and Temple altars had). מִזְבְּחִי (mizbekhi) = "my altar" — the possessive suffix is significant: the altar belongs to God, not to the worshipper. עֶרְוָה (ervah) = "nakedness, exposed genitals, shame, sexual exposure" — from ערה (arah, "to be bare, to uncover"). Ervah is the standard term for sexual exposure in the Levitical code (Lev 18 uses it repeatedly: "you shall not uncover the ervah of..."). The concern is that climbing steps in a robe or tunic would expose the genitals to those below or to the altar itself. לֹא תִגָּלֶה (lo tigaleh) = niphal imperfect of גלה (galah, "to uncover, reveal, expose") — "shall not be uncovered/exposed." The modesty regulation reflects a broader theological principle: the worship of YHWH must not involve the sexual display that characterized Canaanite fertility cults. The naked body is not shameful per se, but its exposure in the context of worship violates the dignity of the sacred encounter. The priestly garments later prescribed (Ex 28:42) include מִכְנְסֵי בָד (mikhnesei vad, "linen undergarments") specifically to cover the ervah — a solution that addresses the same concern. LXX: οὐκ ἀναβήσῃ ἐν ἀναβαθμίσιν ἐπὶ τὸ θυσιαστήριόν μου, ὅπως ἂν μὴ ἀποκαλύψῃς τὴν ἀσχημοσύνην σου ἐπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ("you shall not go up by steps upon my altar, that you may not uncover your shame upon it") — ἀναβαθμίς ("step, stair") for ma'alot; ἀσχημοσύνη ("shame, nakedness, indecency") for ervah — the LXX interprets ervah as "shame/indecency" (ἀσχημοσύνη), a more euphemistic rendering; ἀποκαλύπτω ("uncover, reveal") for tigaleh.

## Translator's Afterword

The Decalogue is the constitutional foundation of the covenant between YHWH and Yisra'el. Its structure moves from identity (v. 2: "I am YHWH") through exclusive relationship (vv. 3–6: no other gods, no images), through the sanctity of the name (v. 7) and the sanctity of time (vv. 8–11), through the bridge of parental honor (v. 12), to the protection of life (v. 13), marriage (v. 14), freedom (v. 15), judicial integrity (v. 16), and the interior life (v. 17). The movement is from God to neighbor, from external act to internal desire, from the public to the private, from the visible to the invisible. The altar law (vv. 22–26) that follows immediately is not an afterthought but a crucial complement: having heard the divine voice, how shall the people worship? With simplicity — earthen altars, uncut stones, no steps, no display. The God who speaks from thick darkness and fire is to be approached with humility, not with architectural grandeur or human craftsmanship. The entire chapter testifies to a God who is simultaneously terrifyingly transcendent (the arafel, the fire, the earthquake) and intimately personal (the "I" who says "you," the singular address throughout the Decalogue, the promise "I will come to you and bless you").
